extendedContent.content:
Laevateinn (Old Norse: "Damage Twig" or "Wounding Wand") is one of the most
                    enigmatic weapons in Norse mythology, mentioned only in the Eddic poem Fjolsvinnsmal.
                    This mysterious weapon was forged by Loki (called Lopt in the poem) and is kept
                    locked in a chest guarded by Sinmara, often identified as the wife of the fire
                    giant Surtr.
                
                
                    The exact nature of Laevateinn is debated by scholars. The name suggests either
                    a wand or staff ("damage twig"), but many interpretations and later traditions
                    present it as a sword. Some scholars believe it may be the same weapon as Surtr's
                    flaming sword, which will be used to destroy the world at Ragnarok.
                
                
                    What makes Laevateinn particularly significant is its stated purpose: it is the
                    only weapon capable of killing Vidofnir, the golden rooster that sits atop the
                    world tree Yggdrasil (or, in some interpretations, another cosmic tree called
                    Mimameidr). This specificity suggests the weapon was created for a particular
                    cosmic purpose.
extendedContent.title: Description and Nature

extendedContent.content:
Laevateinn appears in only one source: the Eddic poem Fjolsvinnsmal (The Lay of
                    Fjolsvinnr), which takes the form of a question-and-answer dialogue. In this poem,
                    the hero Svipdag questions the giant Fjolsvinnr about the mysteries of a fortress
                    and its surroundings.
                
                
                    The relevant passage describes Laevateinn as the only weapon that can kill Vidofnir:
extendedContent.title: Source: Fjolsvinnsmal

extendedContent.content:
Surtr's Flaming Sword
                
                    Some scholars identify Laevateinn with the flaming sword that Surtr will wield at
                    Ragnarok to destroy the gods and set the world on fire. If Sinmara is indeed Surtr's
                    wife, it makes sense that she would guard her husband's weapon. The idea that Loki
                    made this weapon aligns with his role as the instigator of Ragnarok.
                

                Mistletoe Theory
                
                    Another interpretation connects Laevateinn to the mistletoe that killed Baldr.
                    Loki fashioned the mistletoe dart that Hodr used to kill the beloved god. The
                    name "damage twig" could describe this small but deadly weapon. This theory
                    positions Laevateinn as an instrument of Loki's betrayal.
                

                Magical Staff
                
                    Taking the name literally as "wounding wand," some interpret Laevateinn as a
                    magical staff or scepter rather than a sword. Such an object would fit Loki's
                    sorcerous nature better than a conventional blade and aligns with the tradition
                    of powerful magical implements in Norse mythology.
                

                Symbol of Inevitable Doom
                
                    From a mythological perspective, Laevateinn may represent the inevitability of
                    cosmic destruction. Just as Vidofnir cannot be killed without it, the destruction
                    of the world at Ragnarok cannot occur without Loki's machinations. The circular
                    paradox of obtaining the weapon reflects the inescapable nature of fate.
extendedContent.title: Scholarly Interpretations

extendedContent.content:
Despite its obscurity in the original sources, Laevateinn has become popular in
                    modern fantasy and gaming:
                
                
                    Fate Series: Features Laevateinn as a powerful flaming sword,
                    one of the more prominent modern depictions
                    Final Fantasy: Various entries include Laevateinn as a weapon
                    Shin Megami Tensei: Features the weapon in connection with
                    Norse mythology references
                    Various Anime/Manga: Often depicted as a flaming sword or
                    magical weapon associated with fire and destruction
                    Fantasy Literature: Appears in numerous Norse-inspired
                    fantasy works, usually as a sword of fire
                
                
                    Most modern depictions interpret Laevateinn as a flaming sword, reflecting the
                    connection to Surtr and Ragnarok rather than the "wand" interpretation suggested
                    by the original name.
extendedContent.title: Modern Depictions

                    paradox of obtaining the weapon reflects the inescapable nature of fate.

name: Laevateinn
longDescription:
The Damage Twig - Loki's Mysterious Weapon of Destruction Laevateinn (Old Norse: "Damage Twig" or "Wounding Wand") is one of the most
                    enigmatic weapons in Norse mythology, mentioned only in the Eddic poem Fjolsvinnsmal.
                    This mysterious weapon was forged by Loki (called Lopt in the poem) and is kept
                    locked in a chest guarded by Sinmara, often identified as the wife of the fire
                    giant Surtr.
shortDescription: The Damage Twig - Loki's Mysterious Weapon of Destruction

sources.name: The Poetic Edda
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems, the primary source for many Norse myths.
sources.title: The Poetic Edda

sources.name: The Prose Edda by Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A 13th-century Icelandic textbook on Norse mythology.
sources.title: The Prose Edda by Snorri Sturluson

sources.name: Hilda Ellis Davidson, Gods and Myths of Northern Europe
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Norse mythology.
sources.title: Hilda Ellis Davidson, Gods and Myths of Northern Europe

sources.name: John Lindow, Norse Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Heroes, Rituals, and Beliefs
sources.description: An academic guide to Norse mythology.
sources.title: John Lindow, Norse Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Heroes, Rituals, and Beliefs

sources.name: Rudolf Simek, Dictionary of Northern Mythology
sources.description: A scholarly dictionary of Norse mythology terms and figures.
sources.title: Rudolf Simek, Dictionary of Northern Mythology

powers: Only weapon capable of killing Vidofnir
description: The Damage Twig - Loki's Mysterious Weapon of Destruction

keyMyths.title: The Forging of Laevateinn
keyMyths.description: Although not explicitly detailed in surviving texts, some interpretations suggest Laevateinn was forged by Loki in a complex process, possibly involving dark magic and rare materials, to serve as a weapon of immense destructive power. The weapon's creation is shrouded in mystery, adding to its ominous reputation.
keyMyths.source: Speculative interpretations based on kenning and contextual analysis of related myths.

keyMyths.title: Laevateinn and the Eagle's Nest
keyMyths.description: In some accounts, Laevateinn is associated with an eagle perched atop the World Tree, Yggdrasil. The weapon may be used to dislodge the eagle or target its vulnerable spot, potentially symbolizing a challenge to the established order of the cosmos.
keyMyths.source: Interpretations of the eagle's symbolism and its connection to cosmic events in Norse mythology.

keyMyths.title: The Weapon Against Surtr
keyMyths.description: While not explicitly stated, some theories propose that Laevateinn could be a potential weapon against Surtr, the fire giant destined to bring about Ragnarok. Its destructive capabilities align with the scale of the final battle, suggesting a role in combating the forces of chaos.
keyMyths.source: Theories based on weapon symbolism and the overarching narrative of Ragnarok.

keyMyths.title: Laevateinn as a Symbol of Loki's Rebellion
keyMyths.description: Laevateinn can be interpreted as a symbol of Loki's rebellious nature and his desire to disrupt the established order of the gods. As Loki is known for his chaotic and destructive tendencies, Laevateinn becomes an extension of his will, embodying his defiance.
keyMyths.source: Scholarly analysis of Loki's character and his role in Norse cosmology.

keyMyths.title: Laevateinn's Hidden Power
keyMyths.description: The exact nature of Laevateinn's power remains ambiguous, but it is consistently depicted as a weapon of significant destructive potential. Its power might stem from fire, poison, or some other form of dark magic, making it a formidable and feared artifact.
keyMyths.source: General consensus based on the weapon's description and associated imagery.
